exemplar.title: The aim of this exploration is to gather measurements about the speed and distance travelled when skating and to model the area of a speed skate boot.

detailedExemplarData.title: The aim of this exploration is to gather measurements about the speed and distance travelled when skating and to model the area of a speed skate boot.

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical development from introduction through data collection, modelling and conclusion
Well-ordered structure with headings guiding the reader through tables, figures and calculations
Effective integration of GeoGebra figure (Figure 5) showing point plotting and image scaling
weaknesses: Abrupt shifts between sections (e.g. wheel speed to boot-area) without sufficient bridging discussion
Figure 1 is not referenced or discussed in the text, reducing its impact
Table 6 and Table 7 suffer from misformatted headers and confusing alignment, hindering readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration exhibits clear coherence and logical progression (band 3), with a solid introduction, body and conclusion.  Organization is generally strong, but occasional long passages of working interrupt flow and some formatting issues prevent an exceptional band 4.  Conciseness is mostly adequate but redundancies and irrelevant screenshots place it in the upper band 3.

strengths: Correct use of key mathematical symbols (circumference, integrals, percentage error) in many places
Effective inclusion of multiple representations (tables, graphs, annotated photographs, GeoGebra screenshots)
Clear description of image scaling process in GeoGebra
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (missing 'dx' in integrals, variable mismatches, mis-stated speed units)
Numerous OCR and formatting errors in tables (unreadable symbols, inconsistent decimal and unit formatting)
Lack of domain information in some tables and unclear subscript notation without a legend
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student employs mostly appropriate mathematical language and effective representations (band 3 strands), persistent inconsistenciesâmisplaced differential notation, variable naming mismatches and OCR artefactsâundermine clarity.  These weaknesses warrant a band 2 overall for communication, as the work does not achieve the consistency required for band 3.

strengths: Investigation driven by personally collected Strava data, showing significant independent thinking
Clear personal approach in choosing to model own skating equipment and compare numerical methods
Demonstrated creativity and initiative using GeoGebra to fit piecewise polynomials and compare two area methods
weaknesses: Mathematical techniques, while chosen personally, follow standard classroom approaches rather than outstandingly novel ones
Limited extension of personal context into deeper or broader exploration beyond the core tasks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows clear evidence of personal engagement through the studentâs own data and choice of models (band 2).  Independent thinking and creativity are notable but not at the exceptional level required for band 3.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on the under-estimation by the trapezoidal rule and calculation of percentage error
Critical comparison of numerical techniques and identification of sources of error (fit accuracy, number of trapezoids)
Clear linkage of reflection to understanding (e.g. fatigue effects on speed data)
weaknesses: Reflection remains descriptive without deep critique of data quality or modelling assumptions
No discussion of GPS measurement error or error propagation when combining estimates
Limited proposals for further investigation or extensions to strengthen critical reflection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides meaningful reflection and some critical analysis, linking results back to their understanding (band 2).  However, the depth and critical breadth fall short of the substantial, probing reflection required for band 3.

strengths: Relevant SL-level mathematics applied (circumference calculations, piecewise polynomials, integrals, numerical methods)
Effective use of the trapezoidal rule and integral methods with close agreement between results
Good understanding shown in explanations of numerical techniques and polynomial fitting
weaknesses: Minor inaccuracies in coefficients (misplaced constant term), rounding errors and inconsistent conversions
Lack of justification for choice of polynomial degrees and absence of measurement uncertainty discussion
Arithmetic and formatting errors in several tables (typo in h(x), omitted area summation justification, OCR coefficient glitches)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics is largely correct and relevant at SL level (band 4 for relevance), but minor errors in execution and explanation limit correctness and understanding to band 3.  The combination of mostly correct computations with some misunderstandings and lack of rigour places this criterion squarely in band 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing a personally collected Strava data set and deciding to model the boot outline show **significant independent thinking** beyond a textbook example.  The mathematics itself follows standard techniques, so the evidence is solid but not outstanding, corresponding to band 2.

Explanations of the trapezoidal rule, integral set-up and polynomial fitting demonstrate **good understanding** in several sections.  Some steps are accepted without justification (e.g. FitPoly outputs), and discussion of model limitations is brief, so the understanding is solid but not thorough or rigorous, positioning the strand in band 3.

Most computations give reasonable values and the two area methods agree closely; however, minor inaccuracies appear (e.g. speed converted inconsistently, slight mis-copying of coefficients, rounding errors).  Thus the mathematics is **mostly correct with small errors**, matching band 3.

Almost all material included is relevant to modelling skate performance or boot area, yet the write-up repeats information (e.g. wheel circumference shown in both prose and table) and includes several screenshots that do not add new insight.  Explanations of simple conversions are verbose, so the work is **mostly relevant but not consistently concise**, fitting band 2.

The student employs correct symbols for circumference, integrals, units and percentage error, and labels functions appropriately.  Inconsistencies occur (e.g. mismatched variable names, missing differential âdxâ in some integrals, speed units mis-stated), preventing fully consistent usage, but overall the **mathematical language is mostly appropriate**.

Explanations generally accompany symbols and diagrams, allowing the reader to follow the reasoning.  Some transitions (e.g. from trapezoidal values to integral set-up) are abrupt and a few labels in tables are unclear, but the overall **communication is clear and mostly consistent**, meriting band 3.

Circumference calculations, unit conversions, construction of piecewise polynomials, use of definite integrals and numerical methods are all **relevant SL-level mathematics applied effectively** to the context.  The variety and correct selection of these techniques support a band 4 for relevance and level.

Tables, graphs, annotated photographs and Geogebra screenshots are used to illustrate data, function fitting and area subdivision, demonstrating **effective multiple representation**.  The representations support the argument but are not especially sophisticated (no dynamic or comparative plots), so band 3 is justified.

The student reflects on the under-estimation by the trapezoidal rule, comments on speed variation over distance and quantifies percentage error, providing **meaningful reflection**.  The discussion is fairly descriptive and does not probe limitations deeply, so band 2 suits the depth shown.

Using Geogebra to fit piecewise polynomials and comparing two numerical methods shows **notable creativity and initiative**.  These ideas are competently executed but remain within familiar technological tools, so the strand fits band 2 rather than the exceptional band 3.

The exploration moves from a clear introduction through data collection, calculations, modelling and a conclusion in a sequence that the reader can follow.  Each section builds on the previous one (e.g.
 wheel calculations â Strava data â boot modelling), showing **clear logical development**.  Minor jumps do appear (such as the abrupt shift from wheel speed to boot-area with limited bridging discussion) so the coherence is not seamless, but the structure is solid enough for band 3 rather than the exemplary band 4.

There is some **critical analysis**: comparison of two numerical techniques, acknowledgment of possible sources of error (number of trapezoids, curve fitting accuracy).  However, the critique is brief and does not evaluate the Strava data reliability or modelling assumptions in depth, keeping the work in band 2.

Reflection clearly links back to understanding, e.g. recognising why additional trapezoids improve accuracy and how fatigue affects 1000 m speed.  The link enhances understanding but is not thoroughly expanded, so a **clear connection** (band 2) rather than a strong, enhanced connection (band 3) is shown.

An introduction, body and conclusion are evident and headings guide the reader through tables, figures and calculations.  Figures are generally referred to in the text and tables are numbered.  However, long passages of working occasionally interrupt the flow and some figures appear twice or with placeholder text, so the organisation, while **well-ordered**, is not âexceptionallyâ smooth.  Hence band 3 is most appropriate.

The student frames the investigation around their own skating equipment and personal curiosity, directing the choice of methods (wheel analysis, trapezoidal rule vs. integration).  This indicates a **clear personal approach that drives the exploration**, aligning with band 2.

annotations.comment: Table 3 lists boot dimensions but omits measurement uncertainty; consider including error margins for personal measurements.
The student frames the investigation with personal background and connection to skating, demonstrating a clear personal approach.
The aim statement is clear but could be more specific by detailing the expected mathematical outcomes or hypotheses.
Figure 1 is included but not sufficiently referenced in the text; introduce and discuss its relevance to the modelling process.
The multiple Strava screenshots are not synthesized into a coherent data summary; consider consolidating key values into a single table for clarity.
The reflection on Strava data reliability and possible measurement error is missing; consider discussing GPS accuracy and human factors.
Table 1 labels units correctly but mixes mm and m later; maintain consistent notation for unit conversions throughout.
When applying $$2\pi r$$, specify the approximation of $$\pi$$ used to justify the given decimal accuracy.
The equation setting for seconds per revolution is unclear; explicitly define variables and show algebraic steps with units.
In Table 2, the method for averaging âtime per revolutionâ is not justified; clarify whether a weighted average or simple mean is appropriate.
The graph of speed versus time lacks axis labels and units; ensure each figure includes clear, complete labels.
The observation of speed drop at 1000 m reflects meaningful reflection linking data to fatigue effects.
The description of image scaling in GeoGebra is clear and demonstrates effective mathematical communication.
When plotting points for the boot outline, explain the selection criteria and how many points ensure sufficient accuracy.
Figure 5 is well integrated, showing the GeoGebra import and point plotting clearly.
The narrative on the FitPoly function lacks justification for choosing polynomial degrees; discuss why linear versus quadratic was selected.
Table 4 contains a typo in the definition of $$h(x)$$ where the constant term is misplaced; check the equation for accuracy.
Table 8 contains unreadable symbols and formatting errors from OCR; ensure all values are legible and consistently presented.
Explicitly stating the value of h=2 cm for gap 2 calculations effectively clarifies the trapezoidal method.
The inline trapezoidal expression for gap 3 is correctly applied and calculated.
Table 9 omits domain information for the x-values in the gap 3 calculation; include explicit domains when integrating segments.
In Table 10, the integrand for NâOâPâQ uses $$0.023x^2$$ but earlier defined cubic; verify consistency between polynomial and integral.
The total area given as 245.36 cmÂ² should be justified by recounting the sum of segment areas; check for arithmetic errors.
The subtraction of gap areas could be strengthened by discussing error propagation when combining estimates from two methods.
Table 12 integrals for gap 2 show possible OCR errors in coefficients; verify each function before integration.
The discrepancy between total gap area 21.48 cmÂ² here and 21.92 cmÂ² earlier should be analyzed to strengthen reflection.
Ensure that domain endpoints align without gaps or overlaps to maintain continuity of the boot outline.
In Table 5, notation for subscripts and numbering (e.g., $$L_1, K_1$$) is unclear; include a legend or key.
Table 6 is misformatted and headers are confusing; reformat so x and y columns are clearly labeled with values.
The description of the trapezoidal rule is correct but could be supported by a general summation formula for clarity.
The trapezoidal calculation presents a numeric result without units; include âcmÂ²â to indicate area.
Table 7 header âAâ is not clearly defined and cell alignment is inconsistent; revise table for better readability.
The diagram of gap 1 trapezoids is useful, but annotate the width âh=3 cmâ directly on the figure.
Decimal formatting in the integrated subtraction (223.88 cmÂ²) should be consistent with earlier numeric conventions.
The discussion on the underestimation by the trapezoidal rule and computed difference of 4.88 cmÂ² shows solid reflection.
The percentage error formula is applied correctly, but clarify which value is considered the true value in the denominator.
Rounding the percentage error to 2.18% demonstrates precision and effective use of mathematical communication.
The conclusion summarizes results well but would benefit from proposing extensions or further investigation steps.
The conclusion mentions Strava data but lacks critique of data quality; include reflections on data limitations.
